title: Heat loss at party walls and flank walls
subtitle: Why end-of-terrace and semi-detached homes lose more heat — and what to do about it
excerpt: An end-of-terrace house has one or two more exposed walls than a mid-terrace, typically adding 25-40% to its space-heating demand for a comparable floor area. The flank wall and the party wall where it meets an unheated void both need attention in a whole-house retrofit.
sourceNotes: BRE Housing Energy Efficiency report for heat loss by dwelling type. BEIS UK Housing Energy Fact File 2022 for end-of-terrace vs mid-terrace energy comparisons. AECB Warm House Retrofit Guide on flanking losses. Gov.uk Part L1B for U-value targets on existing exposed walls.

glossaryTerms.definition: The rate of heat transfer through a building element, W/m2K. An uninsulated solid brick wall is 2.1 W/m2K; a cavity wall with blown insulation is around 0.5 W/m2K.
A high-conductivity path through the building fabric, typically at junctions between elements, such as where a flank wall meets a cold loft void.
Insulation added to a solid (non-cavity) wall, applied internally or externally.

content.text: A mid-terrace house shares its two long walls with neighbours who heat their homes. Those shared walls conduct little heat to the outside. An end-of-terrace house has one exposed flank wall facing outside air, adding roughly 20-30 m2 of building fabric to the heat-loss calculation. At an uninsulated solid-brick

of 2.1 W/m2K, a 25 m2 flank wall loses around 3,700 kWh/year more than the equivalent internal wall in a mid-terrace, worth approximately a modest cost on gas at 6p/kWh (2025/26 cap rates). Insulating the flank wall to 0.5 W/m2K cuts that loss by 75%.
On a cold morning, touch the interior face of each external wall. The flank wall will feel noticeably colder than the party walls if it is solid and uninsulated. A
makes the difference visible: the flank wall will appear blue on an internal thermal image while the party walls appear neutral-yellow.

Flank walls built after around 1920 are usually cavity construction and can be insulated by blown cavity fill (the same process as a front or rear cavity wall). Pre-1920 flank walls are usually solid: one or two brick thicknesses, no cavity: and need internal or external
. To determine which you have: tap the wall. A dull thud is solid; a hollow sound is cavity. Alternatively, an energy assessor's inspection or an EPC survey will identify the construction type.
At the top of the flank wall, where it meets the loft void, heat conducts through the uninsulated masonry from the warm room below into the cold attic air. This is a linear

along the entire width of the building. Adding loft insulation improves the ceiling U-value but leaves this junction exposed unless the insulation wraps down over the wall top inside the loft to 150-200 mm depth. Check your loft insulation at the wall perimeter and stuff any visible gap with offcuts.
Party walls between heated and unheated spaces

A party wall shared with an adjacent property in the same ownership but used as storage or a garage (unheated) behaves like an external wall: it loses heat from the warm house into the unheated space next door. Insulate the party wall the same as any external wall. If the adjacent property is owned by a different party, you have no obligation to coordinate (the insulation goes on your side, inside your property).

A 1930s end-of-terrace: flank wall 8 m x 5 m = 40 m2 exposed surface (minus windows), cavity construction 1930s. Cavity fill (blown mineral wool): cost around a modest cost reduces U-value from 1.5 to 0.45 W/m2K, saves roughly 2,100 kWh/year on gas heating, worth a modest cost. Payback approximately 5 years. Compare to the mid-terrace neighbour whose equivalent party wall saves nothing because it is shared with a heated space.
